Whenever I feel stuck, I add structure to my days. Map out what you’re going to do for a day in 30 minute increments. It doesn’t have to be the “right” stuff. It just needs to be something. Then stick to it for an entire day. You’ll feel in control and create momentum. It works.

Movement is free therapy. I’ve never gone out for a long run, walk, or lift and not felt better about whatever issue I was stressing over. A lot of problems in life are solved by just moving your body.

The #1 VP of Sales hire to avoid: Anyone who left sales for 1+ years to do something else (influencer, small biz owner, etc.). Sales is a hard, grueling job. Stepping away for too long almost always makes them fail when they come back.

A plumbing supply company had techs writing daily job summaries in Slack. 45 min each, every day. 12 techs = 9 hours of wasted billable time.

We wired Zapier to pull their field-service app entries, push them through Claude, and auto-draft the Slack post. Tech reviews, edits 2-3

Built an intake form for a service business last week. 47 fields across 4 PDFs. Customers were dropping off at 30%.

Replaced it with one conversational flow that auto-fills from address + phone lookups. Submission rate: 71%.

Not AI magic. Just caring that the form was terrible.

3 signs your business is ready for AI (and 3 signs it's not):

Ready:
- You have a repeatable process that eats 10+ hours/week
- Your data lives in structured formats (even messy ones)
- You can define "success" with a number

Not ready:
- "We want AI" but can't name the problem
